body.para: GuestMemory (memoryLib) is the single physical-memory interface -- a passive byte-store with no opinion about devices or faults. It pages the guest's physical address space in on demand, in 64 KiB pages, committing a page only when the guest first touches it. So a machine configured for gigabytes costs only its working set on the host. Pages are backed by VirtualAlloc on Windows and mmap on Linux / ARM hosts (with a portable fallback). The earlier SafeMemory layer has been removed.
The arbiter owns routing, not memory

GuestMemory does no MMIO routing and raises no faults. The TsunamiChipset system-bus arbiter decodes every physical address first: it routes device traffic to the right model, services the chipset CSRs, and raises range faults for non-existent memory before raw RAM is ever touched. That separation is what keeps device / MMIO traffic from ever aliasing real RAM.

The amount of RAM the guest sees is set by the platform manifest and configured into the Cchip's Array Address Registers (AAR). The Tsunami Cchip supports up to four arrays of up to 1 GB each (4 GB maximum); accesses above the configured limit are Non-eXistent Memory (NXM). The configured size is what the firmware reports under show memory and in the configuration table. [TODO: confirm the shipped DS10 default array size against the current build.]
A LockMonitor tracks load-locked / store-conditional reservations per CPU at cache-line (64-byte) granularity, for up to four CPUs -- the substrate for the EV6 LDx_L / STx_C primitives.
